prefix:
Thine eyes I love, and they, as pitying me,
Knowing thy heart torment me with disdain,
Have put on black, and loving mourners be,
target:
Looking with pretty ruth upon my pain.
And truly not the morning sun of heaven
Better becomes the gray cheeks of the east,
Nor that full star that ushers in the even
Doth half that glory to the sober west
As those two mourning eyes become thy face.
O, let it then as well beseem thy heart
To mourn for me, since mourning doth thee grace,
And suit thy pity like in every part.
  Then will I swear beauty herself is black,
  And all they foul that thy complexion lack.
hypothesis:
The best they shall seem, and their fair,
The best they shall shine in my eyes.
  Let the darkness of the blood of the sweetest sweet.
  If thy dear sins make thee sad,
  Let thy blood be meekly mine.
  Then, from my love, I can bear no worse.
  No poor soul shall suffer thy sweet smile.
  Then thou alone, and never again shalt be,
  Though thy love of thy dear sweetheart shall be.
  But thou art my sweetest son, and my love shall never be.
  Be thou my sweetest son, my sweetest son,
  And thou shalt show me the way to heaven.
  Then I shall live and thou shalt live.   I shall never die,
  The night I die shall stay, I shall live in thy presence.
  But I shall live, and thou shalt live.    And thou

prefix:
Beshrew that heart that makes my heart to groan
For that deep wound it gives my friend and me.
Is 't not enough to torture me alone,
target:
But slave to slavery my sweet'st friend must be?
Me from myself thy cruel eye hath taken,
And my next self thou harder hast engrossed;
Of him, myself, and thee I am forsaken,
A torment thrice threefold thus to be crossed.
Prison my heart in thy steel bosom's ward,
But then my friend's heart let my poor heart bail.
Whoe'er keeps me, let my heart be his guard;
Thou canst not then use rigor in my jail.
  And yet thou wilt, for I, being pent in thee,
  Perforce am thine, and all that is in me.
hypothesis:
Or do my bosom weep out grief in their soul,
If my heart, though it may be my bed, were not mine;
Or at least, I think it to be mine, too, though my bosom be.
But, dear friend, no more should I hear
For my heart is well set up, but it is not this.
  And though my heart may not cry out to do
  For that heart, though it shall be mine, it is not so.
  That which is left to do is due to this,
  And if I should die I do not save that which is of my kind.
  I should leave it to these others to make my heart grow.
  I should not leave my own heart alone,  But I should not be too bitter,
  For I am, in my love, my love's love, my love's love.

prefix:
So, now I have confessed that he is thine
And I myself am mortgaged to thy will,
Myself I'll forfeit, so that other mine
target:
Thou wilt restore to be my comfort still.
But thou wilt not, nor he will not be free,
For thou art covetous, and he is kind;
He learned but surety-like to write for me
Under that bond that him as fast doth bind.
The statute of thy beauty thou wilt take,
Thou usurer that put'st forth all to use,
And sue a friend came debtor for my sake;
So him I lose through my unkind abuse.
  Him have I lost; thou hast both him and me.
  He pays the whole, and yet am I not free.

And inherit my new bounty, so that my name may never be
Which is yours, unless it be me.
For thou shalt not lend me the praise of praise,
For I am my chief guest, my most loving servant,
And though thou were my guest, I never give thee praise.
  To thee I say, 'Thou art not my guest.
  Yet my countenance which bears you my love

  And I see thee grow and grow, and wilt die.   To thee I say, 'Thou art my guest.'

prefix:
Whoever hath her wish, thou hast thy will,
And will to boot, and will in overplus.
More than enough am I that vex thee still,
target:
To thy sweet will making addition thus.
Wilt thou, whose will is large and spacious,
Not once vouchsafe to hide my will in thine?
Shall will in others seem right gracious,
And in my will no fair acceptance shine?
The sea, all water, yet receives rain still,
And in abundance addeth to his store;
So thou, being rich in will, add to thy will
One will of mine to make thy large will more.
  Let no unkind, no fair beseechers kill.
  Think all but one, and me in that one will.
hypothesis:
Because the reproach of thy shame is too strong,
And my mind shall live on many days of mourning.
Thy fondest father shall be gone,
And mine love shall be forgotten;
Nor, as he hath been, shall my life ever be.
  But I shall live, in thy beauty,
  and love thy soul;
  And, in my love, shall thy kindness and kindness shine.
  Then my love shall be remembered, and thy love shall be remembered,
  And my love, too, will be remembered.  I shall not feel the love of thee,
  And my love, too, will be remembered.   But that love, thy love, will be remembered.
  For no other kind of love can my heart,

prefix:
If thy soul check thee that I come so near,
Swear to thy blind soul that I was thy will,
And will, thy soul knows, is admitted there.
target:
Thus far for love my love-suit, sweet, fulfill.
Will will fulfill the treasure of thy love,
Ay, fill it full with wills, and my will one.
In things of great receipt with ease we prove
Among a number one is reckoned none.
Then in the number let me pass untold,
Though in thy store's account I one must be.
For nothing hold me, so it please thee hold
That nothing me, a something, sweet, to thee.
  Make but my name thy love, and love that still,
  And then thou lovest me, for my name is Will.
hypothesis:
If thou art a liar, or a thief,
If thou hath committed thyself
To thyself for my sake,
That all that was in thy sight is left.
Thy boast then, let him say that thee
Is that thy self which lies hid,
And thyself where thou liest hid.
  I, then, thou art what I am;

  But this I will not be, unless thou dost leave

  Then I will deny thee.

prefix:
Thou blind fool, Love, what dost thou to mine eyes
That they behold and see not what they see?
They know what beauty is, see where it lies,
target:
Yet what the best is take the worst to be.
If eyes, corrupt by overpartial looks,
Be anchored in the bay where all men ride,
Why of eyes' falsehood hast thou forged hooks,
Whereto the judgment of my heart is tied?
Why should my heart think that a several plot
Which my heart knows the wide world's common place?
Or mine eyes, seeing this, say this is not,
To put fair truth upon so foul a face?
  In things right true my heart and eyes have erred,
  And to this false plague are they now transferred.
hypothesis:
And they see that it is not what is seen but what is seen,
For thou shalt see beauty in what thou lookest.
For as soon as thou lookest, thou shalt see beauty;
And if thou lookest, thou shalt see beauty in that which thou lookest.
  Then thou shalt see what beauty dost thou look,

prefix:
When my love swears that she is made of truth
I do believe her though I know she lies,
That she might think me some untutored youth,
target:
Unlearned in the world's false subtleties.
Thus vainly thinking that she thinks me young,
Although she knows my days are past the best,
Simply I credit her false-speaking tongue;
On both sides thus is simple truth suppressed.
But wherefore says she not she is unjust?
And wherefore say not I that I am old?
O, love's best habit is in seeming trust,
And age in love loves not to have years told.
  Therefore I lie with her and she with me,
  And in our faults by lies we flattered be.
hypothesis:
Wherea I find her to be a false lover.
She would not lie but prove me wrong,
And I do not consider her true or untrue,
Nor do I consider her true or false a fair charge.
  But what false truth in me would you deny,
  But I say that I love not true or false,
  As false as false when you say, 'I love you, dear, with love.'
   And I cannot deny that you are true or false,
  For I love you with my heart, and with my heart with your love.
  And so do I love thee with thy love, that you may prove true,
  For love lies with thy love, and truth with thy truth.
  And I do not love you with thy love, nor do I love thee with thy truth,
  For thou, dear, cannot love me true,
  If thou wouldst think me false or false

target:
Use power with power, and slay me not by art.
Tell me thou lov'st elsewhere; but in my sight,
Dear heart, forbear to glance thine eye aside.
What need'st thou wound with cunning when thy might
Is more than my o'erpressed defense can bide?
Let me excuse thee: ah, my love well knows
Her pretty looks have been mine enemies;
And therefore from my face she turns my foes,
That they elsewhere might dart their injuries.
  Yet do not so; but since I am near slain,
  Kill me outright with looks, and rid my pain.
hypothesis:
Thy poison is so potent, so deadly,
That thou dost fear to well-editating words,
That this mortal wound that is sure
When thou turns thy eye on me is in vain;
Thy poison, therefore, be it with my tears,
Or thou, by my love's pain, will have to die
When my love's death is laid waste;
For my love's most holy poison, though a second stroke
That puts me in this state, hath died.
  Thy poison, therefore, be it with me,
  To make thy thought be so much worse.

prefix:
Be wise as thou art cruel; do not press
My tongue-tied patience with too much disdain,
Lest sorrow lend me words, and words express
target:
The manner of my pity-wanting pain.
If I might teach thee wit, better it were,
Though not to love, yet, love, to tell me so,
As testy sick men, when their deaths be near,
No news but health from their physicians know.
For if I should despair, I should grow mad,
And in my madness might speak ill of thee.
Now this ill-wresting world is grown so bad,
Mad slanderers by mad ears believed be.
  That I may not be so, nor thou belied,
  Bear thine eyes straight, though thy proud heart go wide.
hypothesis:
Of what I do not believe.
Thou art my most cruel foe, my most cruel foe,
And yet never speak so very in my face.
For my eyes are true love's eye, which thou never saw
As a mortal pupil to their true self,
And in them most deceived eyes I see me's true self.

target:
Who in despite of view is pleased to dote.
Nor are mine ears with thy tongue's tune delighted,
Nor tender feeling to base touches prone,
Nor taste, nor smell, desire to be invited
To any sensual feast with thee alone.
But my five wits nor my five senses can
Dissuade one foolish heart from serving thee,
Who leaves unswayed the likeness of a man,
Thy proud heart's slave and vassal wretch to be.
  Only my plague thus far I count my gain,
  That she that makes me sin awards me pain.

prefix:
Love is my sin, and thy dear virtue hate,
Hate of my sin, grounded on sinful loving.
O, but with mine compare thou thine own state,
target:
And thou shalt find it merits not reproving.
Or if it do, not from those lips of thine,
That have profaned their scarlet ornaments
And sealed false bonds of love as oft as mine,
Robbed others' beds' revenues of their rents.
Be it lawful I love thee as thou lov'st those
Whom thine eyes woo as mine importune thee;
Root pity in thy heart, that, when it grows,
Thy pity may deserve to pitied be.
  If thou dost seek to have what thou dost hide,
  By self-example mayst thou be denied.
hypothesis:
That thou, my love, thy thy dear love,
Thy eternal love, art no more despised
With my own vanity, or mine, that thou dost have
With mine fair merit to love thyself,
Or love thyself by thy own gift.
  If thou love not me, what are you doing?
  Take the peace of love, by me,
  Then bring me to thy beauty;
  Give me the heart, and let my beauty lie.
  To thee I love, to thee I love;
  To thee I love, to thee I love.
  But if thou love not me, what are you doing?
  As thou knowest not my weakness, what shall I do?
  So my love is no more despised
  Than thy own.   For all thy worth is my own.
   So my love is not my own,  Because thou dost love my weakness.

prefix:
Lo, as a careful huswife runs to catch
One of her feathered creatures broke away,
Sets down her babe, and makes all swift dispatch
target:
In pursuit of the thing she would have stay,
Whilst her neglected child holds her in chase,
Cries to catch her whose busy care is bent
To follow that which flies before her face,
Not prizing her poor infant's discontent;
So runn'st thou after that which flies from thee,
Whilst I, thy babe, chase thee afar behind.
But if thou catch thy hope, turn back to me
And play the mother's part: kiss me, be kind.
  So will I pray that thou mayst have thy will,
  If thou turn back and my loud crying still.
hypothesis:
To her beloved lover, which she may have
While she hears this tale with her eyes fixed.
It was a cruel perversity of mine,
That no little child should live to see him,
Thy eyes, eyes to show their beauty,
To see him that with his dead heart she dies,
And she must, with all her strength, wait
For him to come home, as he would to her.
  To all whom I praise, say,
  A child should live long, and should not be taken,
  For a memory would not be found in me,
  In the child's heart, I can live to see him.
   The eyes of my love that loved me, doth answer thee
